title: Custom Exceptions
subtitle: Create your own exception types for clearer error handling
learning_objectives: Define a custom exception class by inheriting from Exception
Add a custom message to your exception
Understand when custom exceptions improve code clarity
Catch custom exceptions the same way as built-in ones

sections.heading: Defining a Custom Exception
sections.content: You can create your own exception type by defining a class that inherits from Exception. This lets you give errors descriptive names that explain exactly what went wrong in your specific application.
sections.code_output: Transaction failed: Not enough money in account

sections.heading: Custom Exceptions with Extra Information
sections.content: You can make custom exceptions carry extra information by defining an __init__ method. This is useful when the error handler needs more than just a text message to know what to do.
sections.code_output:
Invalid age: -5. Must be between 0 and 120.
Bad value was: -5
sections.note: Custom exceptions are especially helpful in larger programs where you want to distinguish between different kinds of failures in your own code.

exercises.title: Create a Custom Exception
exercises.prompt: Create a custom exception class called TooYoungError that inherits from Exception. Write a function check_age(age) that raises TooYoungError("Must be 18 or older") if age < 18. Test it with ages 16 and 21.
hints: In check_age, if age < 18: raise TooYoungError("Must be 18 or older")
Catch it with except TooYoungError as e:

tests.message: Output contains: Error: Must be 18 or older

exercises.title: Exception with Extra Data
exercises.prompt: Create a class NegativeValueError(ValueError) with __init__(self, value) that sets self.value = value and calls super().__init__(f'Value cannot be negative: {value}'). Raise it if someone calls square_root(-9). Catch it and print both the error message and e.value.
hints: In __init__: self.value = value then super().__init__(f'Value cannot be negative: {value}')
In square_root: if n < 0: raise NegativeValueError(n)
In the except block: print(e) prints the message, e.value is the number

quiz.question: How do you create a custom exception called OutOfStockError?
quiz.explanation: Custom exceptions are Python classes that inherit from Exception (or any built-in exception). The simplest version is just: class MyError(Exception): pass

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of creating custom exception classes?
options: They run faster than built-in exceptions
They can only be used once
They give errors meaningful, app-specific names that make code easier to understand
They prevent the program from crashing
quiz.explanation: Custom exceptions like InsufficientFundsError or TooYoungError are much more descriptive than a generic ValueError. They make code self-documenting — a developer reading except InsufficientFundsError immediately knows what situation is being handled.

completion_message: Excellent! You have completed the Error Handling module! Your programs can now handle failures gracefully and communicate errors clearly.
